chunks.title: Arrays
chunks.text: An array is a contiguous block of memory holding fixed-size elements of the same type. Random access by index is O(1) because the address of element i is base + i*sizeof(elem). Insertion or deletion in the middle is O(n) because subsequent elements have to be shifted. Arrays are the foundation of most other data structures and the default choice when you know the size up front.

chunks.title: Linked Lists
chunks.text: A linked list is a sequence of nodes, each storing a value and a pointer to the next node. Singly-linked lists allow O(1) insert/delete at the head and O(n) random access. Doubly-linked lists store both next and previous pointers, enabling O(1) deletion when you already hold the node. Linked lists are preferred when frequent insertions/deletions matter and random access doesn't.

chunks.title: Stacks
chunks.text: A stack is a LIFO (last-in-first-out) container with two operations: push (add to top) and pop (remove from top), both O(1). Stacks model function calls, expression evaluation, undo/redo, and recursive algorithms. They can be implemented over an array or a linked list. Stack overflow happens when push exceeds capacity; stack underflow when pop is called on an empty stack.

chunks.title: Queues
chunks.text: A queue is a FIFO (first-in-first-out) container with enqueue (add to back) and dequeue (remove from front), both O(1) with a circular array or doubly-linked list. Queues appear in BFS traversal, OS scheduling, request buffering, and producer-consumer patterns. Variants include the deque (insert/remove from both ends) and the priority queue (highest-priority element comes out first, usually implemented as a heap).

chunks.title: Trees and Binary Search Trees
chunks.text: A tree is a hierarchical structure with a root and child nodes. A binary tree has at most two children per node. A binary search tree (BST) maintains the invariant: for every node, left-subtree values < node.value < right-subtree values, enabling O(log n) average search/insert/delete on balanced trees. Worst case is O(n) when the tree degenerates into a line. Self-balancing variants like AVL and red-black trees guarantee O(log n) by rebalancing on each modification.

chunks.title: Heaps and Priority Queues
chunks.text: A heap is a complete binary tree satisfying the heap property: in a max-heap, every parent is >= its children; in a min-heap, every parent is <=. Stored as an array where node i has children at 2i+1 and 2i+2. Insert and extract-max/min are O(log n). Heaps are the standard implementation for priority queues and the core of heap sort. They're used in Dijkstra's algorithm, A* search, and event scheduling.

chunks.title: Hash Tables
chunks.text: A hash table maps keys to values using a hash function that converts a key to an array index. Average lookup/insert/delete is O(1). Collisions are resolved either by chaining (each bucket holds a linked list) or open addressing (probing for the next free slot). Load factor (n/capacity) drives resize: when it gets too high, the table doubles and all keys are rehashed. Hash tables back Python dicts, Java HashMaps, JS objects.

chunks.title: Graphs
chunks.text: A graph is a set of vertices connected by edges. Directed graphs have one-way edges; undirected graphs have two-way edges; weighted graphs label edges with costs. Two main representations: adjacency matrix (n×n, O(1) edge lookup, O(n²) space) and adjacency list (better for sparse graphs, O(V+E) space). Graphs model social networks, road maps, dependency graphs, and the web. Traversal uses DFS (stack/recursion) or BFS (queue).

chunks.title: Tries
chunks.text: A trie (prefix tree) stores strings such that each node represents a character and a path from root to a marked node represents a stored string. Insertion and lookup are O(L) where L is the string length, independent of how many strings are stored. Tries enable fast prefix search (autocomplete), spell check, and IP routing. The space cost is high for sparse alphabets unless compressed (radix tree, Patricia trie).

chunks.title: AVL Trees
chunks.text: An AVL tree is a self-balancing BST where the heights of the two child subtrees of every node differ by at most 1. After each insert/delete, the tree checks the balance factor (height(left) − height(right)) and performs single or double rotations to restore the invariant. AVL guarantees O(log n) operations and tends to be slightly more balanced than red-black trees, making lookups marginally faster at the cost of more rotations on updates.

chunks.title: Red-Black Trees
chunks.text: A red-black tree is a self-balancing BST where each node is coloured red or black such that no red node has a red parent, and every root-to-leaf path has the same number of black nodes. These rules guarantee that the longest path is at most twice the shortest, keeping the tree O(log n) deep. Red-black trees back std::map in C++, TreeMap in Java, and Linux's process scheduler.

chunks.title: B-Trees and B+ Trees
chunks.text: A B-tree is a self-balancing search tree where each node holds many keys and has many children — typically chosen to match a disk block size. This shallow, wide shape minimises disk reads, which is why B-trees and B+ trees back relational database indexes (PostgreSQL, MySQL InnoDB) and file systems (NTFS, HFS+). B+ trees keep data only in the leaves and chain them together, so range scans are sequential.

chunks.title: Skip Lists
chunks.text: A skip list is a probabilistic alternative to balanced BSTs. It layers multiple sorted linked lists where each higher level is a sparse subset of the level below. Search descends from the top, skipping over large ranges. Expected operations are O(log n) by random level assignment. Skip lists are simpler to implement than red-black trees and used in Redis sorted sets and LevelDB's memtable.

chunks.title: Disjoint Set Union (Union-Find)
chunks.text: DSU tracks a partition of elements into disjoint sets, supporting find (which set is x in?) and union (merge two sets). With path compression and union by rank/size, the amortized cost per operation is the inverse Ackermann function — effectively O(1). DSU is the backbone of Kruskal's MST, cycle detection in undirected graphs, and dynamic connectivity problems.

chunks.title: Segment Trees
chunks.text: A segment tree is a balanced binary tree over an array where each node stores an aggregate (sum, min, max, GCD) of a contiguous range. Range queries and point updates both run in O(log n). With lazy propagation, range updates also drop to O(log n). Segment trees are the go-to for competitive programming problems requiring repeated range queries on a mutable array.

chunks.title: Fenwick Tree (Binary Indexed Tree)
chunks.text: A Fenwick tree (BIT) stores prefix sums in an implicit binary structure inside an array. Both point updates and prefix-sum queries run in O(log n). The tricky bit is the `i & -i` trick to isolate the lowest set bit — it tells you the next index to update or read. Fenwick trees are smaller and faster than segment trees when the operation is invertible, like sum or XOR.

chunks.title: Suffix Arrays and Suffix Trees
chunks.text: A suffix array is the sorted array of all suffixes of a string; a suffix tree is a compressed trie of those suffixes. Both enable O(|P|) substring search after O(n log n) or O(n) preprocessing. They power genome alignment, full-text indexes, and the longest-common-substring problem. Suffix arrays use less memory; suffix trees support more operations directly.

chunks.title: Bloom Filters
chunks.text: A Bloom filter is a probabilistic data structure that tests set membership with no false negatives and tunable false positives. It uses k hash functions and a bit array: insert sets k bits; query checks if all k bits are set. Bloom filters are tiny relative to the actual key set, making them ideal for caches (Cassandra, HBase) to skip disk reads when a key is definitely absent.

chunks.title: LRU Cache
chunks.text: An LRU (least-recently-used) cache evicts the entry whose last access is oldest when capacity is exceeded. The classic O(1) implementation combines a doubly-linked list (ordered by recency) with a hash map (key → list node). On access, move the node to the front; on insert at capacity, drop the tail. LRU is the eviction policy behind Redis maxmemory, browser caches, and CPU caches.

chunks.title: Doubly Linked List Operations
chunks.text: A doubly linked list has nodes with both next and prev pointers. Insert and delete at either end are O(1) when you hold a head and tail pointer. Deletion of an arbitrary node is O(1) given the node itself — no traversal needed. The cost is one extra pointer per node and an extra write per modification. Doubly linked lists underpin many LRU caches and OS run-queues.

chunks.title: Circular Buffer (Ring Buffer)
chunks.text: A circular buffer is a fixed-size array used as if its end connects to its beginning. Two indices (head, tail) advance modulo capacity. It supports O(1) enqueue/dequeue and uses no dynamic allocation, which makes it the standard producer-consumer queue in audio, networking, and embedded systems. It's lock-free for single-producer-single-consumer with atomic operations.

chunks.title: Floyd's Cycle Detection
chunks.text: Floyd's tortoise-and-hare algorithm detects a cycle in a linked list (or any functional graph) using two pointers: one moves one step, the other moves two. If they meet, there's a cycle; if the fast pointer hits null, there isn't. Finding the cycle start needs a second pass starting the slow pointer from the head. Runs in O(n) time and O(1) space — far cheaper than hashing every visited node.

chunks.title: Hash Set vs Hash Map
chunks.text: A hash set stores only keys; a hash map stores key→value pairs. Both use a hash function and resolve collisions identically (chaining or open addressing). A set answers 'is x present?' in O(1); a map answers 'what is x associated with?' in O(1). Internally a set is often just a map whose values are unit/null. Use the set when you don't need an associated value — it's slightly leaner.

chunks.title: Treap
chunks.text: A treap is a BST + heap hybrid: each node has a key (BST-ordered) and a randomly chosen priority (heap-ordered). The random priorities make the tree balanced in expectation, giving O(log n) operations. Treaps support split and merge in O(log n), which makes them powerful for implicit-key sequences (insert at position k, reverse a range) — useful in competitive programming.

chunks.title: Persistent Data Structures
chunks.text: A persistent data structure preserves its previous versions on every modification — every update returns a new structure rather than mutating in place. Path copying and structural sharing keep this affordable (e.g., a persistent BST shares unchanged subtrees). They underpin Git's object model, functional language collections (Clojure, Scala), and time-travel undo.

chunks.title: KD-Trees
chunks.text: A KD-tree is a binary tree that recursively partitions k-dimensional space by alternating splitting axes. Insertion and balanced lookup are O(log n) in low dimensions. The classic use case is nearest-neighbour search — for a query point, descend the tree pruning subtrees whose bounding box can't beat the best distance found so far. KD-trees degrade in high dimensions; ball trees and HNSW often beat them past ~20-30 dims.

chunks.title: Sparse Table
chunks.text: A sparse table answers idempotent range queries (min, max, GCD) on a static array in O(1) per query after O(n log n) preprocessing. It stores, for each (i, j), the aggregate of the range starting at i with length 2^j. A query on [l, r] reads two overlapping precomputed ranges. Sparse tables don't support updates, but for queries-only workloads they beat segment trees.

chunks.title: Multiset and Sorted Set
chunks.text: A sorted set (TreeSet, std::set) keeps elements in sorted order and supports O(log n) insertion, deletion, and successor/predecessor queries. A multiset allows duplicates. Both are usually backed by a balanced BST. They're the natural choice for problems that need a sliding ordered view of a stream — median tracking, top-k maintenance, range queries.

chunks.title: Heap Operations Detail
chunks.text: Heap insert: place the new element at the end, then sift up — swap with the parent while the heap property is violated. Extract-max: swap root with the last element, shrink the array, sift down — swap with the larger child while needed. Heapify (build heap from array) is O(n), not O(n log n), because the work is dominated by the lower levels which are mostly leaves.

chunks.title: Cartesian Tree
chunks.text: A Cartesian tree built on a sequence has the BST property over indices and the heap property over values. It can be built in O(n) using a stack-based scan, making it useful for range-minimum-query reductions: RMQ on the array reduces to LCA on the Cartesian tree, which is O(1) with sparse-table preprocessing.

chunks.title: Splay Trees
chunks.text: A splay tree is a self-adjusting BST: every access (search, insert, delete) splays the touched node to the root via a sequence of rotations. This gives O(log n) amortized operations but O(n) worst case for a single op. The benefit is locality — frequently accessed items naturally bubble up, giving great practical performance on skewed access patterns.

chunks.title: Hash Function Quality
chunks.text: A good hash function spreads inputs uniformly over the output range and avoids predictable collisions. Cryptographic hashes (SHA-256) are strong but slow; non-cryptographic hashes (xxHash, FNV, MurmurHash) are fast and adequate for hash tables. Linear-probing tables especially need a hash that scrambles low bits, since the table size is usually a small prime or power of two.

chunks.title: Choosing the Right Structure
chunks.text: The choice usually narrows down by access pattern: array for indexed access; hash map for keyed lookup; sorted set for ordered range queries; heap for repeated min/max extraction; trie for prefix queries; graph for relationships; segment/Fenwick tree for range queries with updates. Match the constant factors and cache behaviour, not just the asymptotic class — a flat array often beats a tree below ~1000 items.

chunks.title: Amortized Analysis
chunks.text: Amortized analysis bounds the average cost per operation in the worst case over a sequence of operations, even when individual operations can be expensive. Three techniques: aggregate (total cost / n ops), accounting (charge each op a fixed amount and credit a savings account), potential (define a potential function on the data structure). Dynamic array doubling and union-find are classic amortized-O(1) examples.
